title: iOS 17.5 Includes 'Repair State' Option That Doesn't Require Turning Off Find My for Service
content:
With iOS 17.5, Apple is adding a "Repair State" feature that is designed to allow an iPhone to be sent in for service without deactivating Find My and Activation Lock. The fourth iOS 17.5 beta that came out today adds a "Remove This Device" option for all devices in ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå, and using it with an ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå puts that ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå into the new Repair State.


Right now, sending an ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå to Apple to be repaired requires turning off Find My, and Apple says that it "might not" be able to repair devices that have ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå activated. Turning off ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå disables Activation Lock, an important anti-theft security feature that prevents an ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå from being used with another Apple ID.

Apple currently uses ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå and Activation Lock as a way to ensure that a person sending a device in for repair actually owns that device, and that it's not stolen. An ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå sent in for repair with ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå disabled cannot be tracked, and it is not protected from theft, so if it is lost or stolen at some point in transit during the repair process, there is no recovery method available. Removing ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå also has a hitch with Stolen Device Protection, as there is an hour wait when turning off ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå, which can be inconvenient for repair purposes.

The new repair state leaves ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå turned on, so Activation Lock remains enabled, and the ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå continues to be trackable with the ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå app while it is being repaired. When enabled, the device in the repair state has a "Ready for Repair" label. "This device remains fully functional in the repair state," reads the text.


In the list of devices, an ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå put into a repair state has a small stethoscope icon, and it can be marked as lost. While in repair state, the ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå is fully functional.

As of right now, using the "Remove This Device" option in ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå to enable a repair state appears to be limited to the ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå. Using it on other devices like an ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå, Mac, or Apple Watch shows a warning that the feature will remove the device from the ‚ÄåApple ID‚Äå account, allowing it to be used by someone else. Note that an ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå must be online and trackable through ‚ÄåFind My‚Äå for the repair state option to pop up. An ‚ÄåiPhone‚Äå that is offline will display the standard removal text. It is also worth noting that repair state cannot be disabled at the current time.

Repair state is a feature that is still in development, so it might work with other devices when iOS 17.5 and its sister updates launch in May.
